title: How to Calculate Debt-to-Income Ratio from Bank Statements
meta: Learn how lenders calculate debt-to-income (DTI) ratio directly from bank statements, including which payments to include, which to exclude, and what DTI thresholds mean.
content:
What Is Debt-to-Income Ratio?

Debt-to-income ratio (DTI) is one of the most important metrics lenders use to evaluate a borrower's ability to take on new debt. It compares monthly debt obligations to monthly gross income, expressed as a percentage.

DTI Formula: Total Monthly Debt Payments ÷ Gross Monthly Income × 100

For example, if someone earns $8,000/month and has $2,400 in monthly debt payments, their DTI is 30%.

Why Bank Statements Are Better Than Self-Reported DTI

When borrowers fill out loan applications, they often underreport their obligations — either intentionally or because they forget smaller payments. Bank statements show every recurring payment leaving the account, making it much harder to misrepresent actual obligations.

Bank-statement-derived DTI captures obligations that don't appear on credit reports, including:

  • Merchant cash advance (MCA) repayments
  • Payments to private lenders
  • Recurring transfers to family members (informal debt obligations)
  • Rent payments (which don't appear on credit reports)
  • Business obligations paid from personal accounts

Step-by-Step: Calculating DTI from Bank Statements

Step 1: Calculate Average Monthly Income

Add up all qualifying deposits over the review period and divide by the number of months. Exclude: transfers between the borrower's own accounts, tax refunds, one-time large deposits, and cash deposits that can't be verified.

Step 2: Identify All Recurring Monthly Obligations

Go through the withdrawals and flag every recurring payment — anything that appears in approximately the same amount on the same schedule every month. Include: loan payments, credit card minimum payments, car payments, insurance premiums, rent, and MCA repayments.

Step 3: Divide and Multiply

Divide total monthly obligations by average monthly income, then multiply by 100 to get the DTI percentage.

What DTI Thresholds Mean for Different Loan Types

  • Conventional mortgage: Maximum 43–45% DTI (28% front-end, 36% back-end preferred)
  • FHA mortgage: Up to 57% DTI allowed with compensating factors
  • VA mortgage: 41% preferred, higher allowed with residual income
  • Business loans: Most lenders want DSCR of 1.25x or better (equivalent to DTI under 80%)
  • Personal loans: Typically 35–43% maximum
  • Auto loans: 45–50% is common maximum

Front-End vs. Back-End DTI

For mortgage lending, DTI is split into two ratios:

  • Front-end DTI (housing ratio): Housing costs only (PITI — principal, interest, taxes, insurance) ÷ gross income. Target: under 28%.
  • Back-end DTI (total debt ratio): All monthly obligations including housing ÷ gross income. Target: under 36–43%.

How MCA Debt Destroys DTI

A merchant cash advance repaid at $350/day is approximately $7,700/month. For a business generating $15,000/month in revenue, that single MCA obligation represents a 51% DTI — often pushing total obligations well above any lender's threshold.

Automating DTI Calculation

Manually identifying every recurring obligation across months of transactions is tedious and error-prone. AI tools like StatementScrub automatically identify recurring payment patterns, calculate average monthly income, and provide a complete DTI analysis in seconds.
